title: The Dazzling Morning Sun that Melts Me
description: Adachi wakes up early and reflects on her life with Shimamura. A quiet, tender morning moment filled with love, vulnerability, and the simple joy of making breakfast.
content:
I woke up earlier than expected—not that it's unusual. If anything, being able to sleep peacefully beside Shimamura now counts as progress. It still amazes me that the old me managed to stay healthy while living the way I used to.

I sat up, yawned, and stared for a moment at the plushies on the shelf.

Last night, I had a little to drink. I glanced at Shimamura, still asleep beside me. Did I smell like alcohol? It only occurred to me now to worry about that.

I quietly slipped out of the bedroom, passed through the living room, and stepped toward the sunlight. The small balcony, with just enough space for two chairs but not enough to stand comfortably, still felt a little cramped even when sitting. I leaned my weight against my usual chair and lifted my face toward the light.

Sunrise and sunset look a bit similar, but the sunrise has a stronger yellow to it. I guess that's the difference between beginnings and endings.

Moon‑gazing in the park at night is nice too, but the sun… the sun pierces my heart so much more deeply. Shimamura is my light—my sun—and that hasn't changed since we were students. If I ever lost her…

Just imagining it made my chest tighten. But if Shimamura were to die right now…

I wouldn't hesitate. If Shimamura were gone, I'd die in the same instant. It wouldn't be some romantic 'I'll follow after her someday.' I simply wouldn't know how to keep living. Shimamura is, in every sense, my life itself.

The thought alone brought tears to my eyes. I wiped them away and looked back toward the light. Through the blur, the sun looked fractured, as though it had split apart. And if there were several Shimamuras like that… what would become of me? I actually found myself considering that ridiculous possibility. There's no way I could love a whole crowd of Shimamuras equally. Just imagining it filled me with a strange, quiet unease.

Then I noticed the dirt on the floor and the railing becoming visible in the sunlight. 'Guess I'll do a deep cleaning today,' I thought.

I went back inside after my little sunbath and started preparing breakfast before Shimamura woke up. She likes fried eggs—just a small portion is enough to make her happy as long as it's on the table. So I've been trying to learn how to fry them better and figure out the right seasoning… but it's harder than it sounds. The real problem is that I don't trust my own sense of taste.

I try to pay attention, but the details always fade too quickly. I know the general idea of how something should taste, but I never know which version Shimamura prefers. Cooking has made me realize, over and over, that she and I are two different people. And every time that hits me, I feel a small, quiet loneliness.

Shimamura says it's good that we're different, though.

And she's right, I guess. There's no point in trying to become exactly like her. Maybe… that's how it should be.

Once everything was ready, I headed back to the bedroom. Shimamura had rolled over in her sleep and kicked off her blanket, leaving her skin exposed in a way my eyes immediately caught. I panicked and hurried to pull the blanket back over her. Only after tucking her in did I realize I'd been holding my breath. No one but me saw her—so why was I this flustered?

It's different from before. I'm used to seeing every part of Shimamura now—more than just that, really—but whenever something like this happens, the calmness I thought I'd finally built up starts to waver again. Maybe I haven't changed at all. No… if it were the old me, I would've bolted out of the room the moment I saw her like that. My mind would've gone blank, and I'd end up crouched in the corner, trembling. In that sense, I've grown—if only a little. I tried, halfheartedly, to give myself credit for that.

And just as I was trying to encourage myself, Shimamura slowly stirred in bed.

'Wah—'

'Mhm… morning.'

She sat up as if she'd forgotten the way she looked, greeting me with sleepy eyes and not even trying to hide anything.

The sunlight hadn't reached the room yet, but her skin still looked bright enough to melt me and then—Crack. My joints complained.

'G‑good morning.'

Click. Something tugged painfully at the back of my neck.

I don't think I'll ever get used to this.

The tremble in my voice spread, and I went back to being the same old me.
